sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Good, LEFT caused a gameplay change with 19 cells changed. Let me check what exactly moved by comparing before and after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after to see what moved
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = after.segmentation['nodes']

print("=== BEFORE ===")
for n in before_seg:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'B', 'w']:  # interesting colors
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\n=== AFTER ===")
for n in after_seg:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'B', 'w']:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see what happened:
- The green box with the white dot (Node 6) moved from [[30, 18], [30, 20], [32, 20], [32, 18]] to [[30, 15], [30, 17], [32, 17], [32, 15]] - it moved LEFT by 3 columns.
- The white dot (Node 9) moved from [31, 19] to [31, 16] - also moved LEFT by 3 columns.
- The white panel (Node 1) gained 1 pixel (290 -> 291), probably filling in where the green box was.
So LEFT moves the green box with the white dot to the left. The green box with the black dot (Node 7) didn't move.
Let me understand the board layout:
- Left panel: white area containing the green box with white dot and a charcoal box
- Middle: gap + purple bar
- Right panel: white area containing the green box with black dot, a white 3x3, and a charcoal box
The green box with the white dot seems to be the player-controlled piece. Let me try moving it around to understand the goal.
Let me check the current state more carefully and try moving it towards the charcoal box or the other green box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The green box with white dot moved LEFT. Let me check the current ASCII to see the new position
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== Current board region ===")
for i, row in enumerate(ascii_lines):
    if 29 <= i <= 33:
        print(f"Row {i:2d}: {row[8:54]}")

# So the player is the green box with white dot. Let me try moving RIGHT to go back
# and explore other directions
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== Current board region ===
Row 29: gwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwwcwwwcwwwww
Row 30: gwwwwwwNNNwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwcccccwwwww
Row 31: gwwwwwwNWNwwwwwwwwwNBNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwww
Row 32: gwwccccNNNwwwwwwwwwNNNwwwppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwww
Row 33: gwwcwwwcwwwwwwwwgggggggggppppppwwwwwwwwwwwwwww

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see the green box with white dot moved left. Now let me understand the layout:
- Left panel has a charcoal box at roughly (32-36, 11-15) and the green box with white dot
- The green box is now at (30-32, 15-17)
- The other green box with black dot is at (30-32, 27-29)
- The right panel has a charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48)
The goal seems to be moving the green box with the white dot to match something. Let me try moving RIGHT to go back, then UP/DOWN to see what's possible.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving RIGHT to go back to original position, then explore UP
# First RIGHT to undo the LEFT
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT result: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Check current position
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green box: boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
RIGHT result: board_changed=True, gameplay_changed=True
Green box: boundary=[[30, 18], [30, 20], [32, 20], [32, 18]], children=[9]
Green box: boundary=[[30, 27], [30, 29], [32, 29], [32, 27]], children=[10]
